1:
"And the child Samuel ministered unto the LORD before Eli. And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision." The witnesses note that the word of the LORD was precious in those days, with no open vision — the crux of the famine of the word of God, the rarity of revelation in a time of declension. On "the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision," interpreters expound the dearth. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the famine of the word — "and the child Samuel ministered unto the LORD before Eli. And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision": in those dark days of Eli's failing priesthood, the word of God was rare and prophetic vision scarce; the truth that where the word is rare the people perish for lack of vision, the light withheld in judgment, "where there is no vision, the people perish," Proverbs 29:18; The figure of the famine of the word of God, the rarity of revelation in a time of declension.

On "And the child Samuel ministered unto the LORD before Eli," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note Samuel serving amid the dearth. Keil and Ellicott note the child Samuel ministering to the LORD under Eli, the one bright servant in the barren time (the servant amid the dearth, Amos 8:11) — the ministry (Samuel ministered).

On "And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the famine of the word. Calvin and Gill note the word of the LORD rare and prophetic vision scarce, God withholding his light in a season of corruption, the dearth a token and judgment of the low estate of religion, the famine of hearing the word (the rarity of the word, Proverbs 29:18; Hebrews 1:1-2) — the figure (the spiritual dearth that God in mercy breaks by raising up a new prophet); the providence (the dearth, the call of Samuel to follow, vv.2-10). The verse displays the rarity of the word of the LORD in those days.

The witnesses agree the substance: in those dark days of Eli's failing priesthood, the word of God was rare and prophetic vision scarce (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the famine of the word: the word of the LORD was precious... there was no open vision — the spiritual barrenness of the time, the word rare and scarce, no frequent or public prophecy, God withholding the light of his revelation in a season of corruption and declension, the rarity both a token and a judgment of the low estate of religion and a foil to the dawn in Samuel's call, the withdrawing of God's word among the heaviest of judgments, a famine of hearing the word (Amos 8:11), the people perishing where there is no vision (Proverbs 29:18), the silence broken at last when God speaks by his Son (Hebrews 1:1-2). The verse displays the famine-of-the-word crux. The rarity of the word of the LORD in those days displays the crux: the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision (Amos 8:11; Proverbs 29:18; Hebrews 1:1-2) — the famine of the word of God, the rarity of revelation in a time of declension, the night before the dawn that God would break by raising up Samuel his prophet. The verse displays Samuel lying down in the temple where the ark was (vv.2-3).

The verse holds together the setting. The verse displays the quiet setting of the scene, the aged Eli with failing sight lying in his place, the old order waning as God prepares to call the young Samuel. The verse displays the truth that the dimming of the old prepares the way for the rising of the new in God's providence.

9:
"Therefore Eli said unto Samuel, Go, lie down: and it shall be, if he call thee, that thou shalt say, Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth. So Samuel went and lay down in his place." The witnesses note Eli teaching Samuel to answer the LORD, Speak, LORD, for thy servant heareth — the crux of the attentive and obedient heart that waits to hear and to do the word of God. On "thou shalt say, Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth," interpreters expound the answer. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the posture of obedient hearing — "therefore Eli said unto Samuel... if he call thee, that thou shalt say, Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth": Eli teaches Samuel the right answer to God's call, the readiness to hear and obey; the truth that we must hear God's word not as critics or masters but as servants ready to obey, "be swift to hear," and "be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only," James 1:19,22; The figure of the attentive and obedient heart that waits to hear and to do the word of God.
